title: Fabric and lace impression coaster set
subtitle: Six round coasters with pressed textile texture
excerpt: A set of six round 90 mm air-dry clay coasters, each pressed with a different textile to capture woven, knotted, or weft textures in the surface. No kiln needed. PVA sealed and felt-backed for everyday use.

glossaryTerms.definition: the stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to carve and join
fully air-dried but unfired clay : fragile and absorbent
unfired clay work at any stage from freshly formed to bone-dry

content.text: Each coaster in this set of six is 90 mm in diameter and 6 mm thick, made from air-dry clay pressed with a different fabric to transfer the woven or knotted texture into the surface. Six different textiles give six distinct surfaces: cotton lace, hessian, fine muslin, ribbed cord, crochet, and rough linen. No kiln is required. The
greenware
is sealed with PVA on all surfaces and felt pads are adhered to the base to protect table surfaces.
Preparation

Cut a 95 mm circle from card to use as a cutting template for all six coasters: the clay shrinks about 5 mm diameter on drying from this template. Gather six fabric samples, each at least 120 mm square, so the texture extends to the edges of the coaster. Fabrics with an open, distinct weave : lace, hessian, ribbed cord fabric, crochet, muslin, and rough linen : produce the clearest impressions. Tightly woven fabrics such as cotton canvas give a fine, even texture that is less readable as a surface pattern. Cut 6 mm spacer guides from card. Have a round cookie cutter or glass 90 mm in diameter available as an alternative to the fettling knife for cutting circles.
Construction

Lay the first fabric sample face-up on the wooden board. Break off approximately 100 g of clay, wedge briefly, and flatten onto the fabric surface. Roll from the centre outward with the rolling pin resting on the 6 mm spacers. The fabric texture will press into the underside of the clay as you roll. Roll to an even thickness the full width of the spacers.
Place the 95 mm card template on the top surface of the clay and cut around it with the fettling knife in a smooth, continuous rotation. Lift the excess clay away from around the cut circle.

Peel the fabric away from the underside of the clay circle. If the fabric sticks, slide the needle tool under the edge to release it, then peel slowly. The impressed texture should be clean and even across the full circle.
Smooth the plain top surface lightly with a barely damp pottery sponge to remove any finger marks from cutting. Do not smooth the textured underside: this is the display face of the finished coaster.
Flip the coaster so the textured face is up. Use the needle tool to smooth any rough clay at the edge of the circle. At the
leather-hard
stage, bevel the edge by drawing the needle tool at 45 degrees around the circumference to remove the sharp cut edge.
Repeat steps 1 to 5 for each of the remaining five fabric samples. Set all six coasters textured face up on a flat wooden board to dry.
Drying
Leave on the flat board for 24 to 2 days at room temperature until
bone-dry

: cool and uniform in colour all over, with no flex when lifted. Round pieces are less prone to warping than squares, but drying near a localised heat source can still cause one edge to lift. If this happens, press gently flat and place a small weighted board on top. Do not force a warped coaster flat after bone-dry : the clay will crack.
Finishing

Seal all surfaces of each bone-dry coaster with undiluted PVA: textured face, smooth back, and edge. Two coats applied 30 minutes apart give sufficient water resistance for a glass of water or a warm cup placed on the surface. The PVA penetrates the clay and hardens it noticeably. Once the second PVA coat is dry and clear, adhere self-adhesive felt pads to the base at four evenly spaced points around the circumference. Felt protects the table and stops the coasters sliding.
Care

Wipe down with a damp cloth. Do not submerge in water or put in a dishwasher: the PVA seal is water-resistant for surface use but not waterproof for prolonged soaking. If the felt pads peel away, replace with new self-adhesive pads. Re-seal with a fresh coat of PVA if the surface begins to feel powdery after extended use.
Variations
Terracotta wash: before sealing, brush a dilute red acrylic wash over the textured face. The wash pools in the weave impressions and reads darker there, giving the coaster a warm earthenware tone.
Rectangular coasters: cut 90 mm by 90 mm squares instead of circles. Score the corners with the fettling knife and a ruler for clean lines.
Inked texture: after sealing with PVA, rub a stamp ink pad lightly across the raised surface. This highlights the weave impression in a contrasting colour.
Roll the clay over the fabric on the smooth top surface as well to create a double-sided textured coaster.

rows.problem: Fabric sticks to clay and tears when peeled
rows.cause: Clay was too wet and pressed into the fabric fibres
rows.fix: Knead the clay until it no longer sticks to your hands before rolling. Roll onto the fabric surface rather than pressing the fabric into the clay from above.

rows.cause: Clay rolled too thick relative to the fabric openness, or insufficient pressure
rows.fix: Re-roll to 6 mm and press the rolling pin down firmly on the final pass. Open-weave fabrics (lace, hessian) give the clearest impressions.

rows.cause: Uneven drying : one side loses moisture faster than the other
rows.fix: Dry on a flat wooden board away from any heat source. If curling starts, flip the coaster textured-face down and weight lightly.

rows.cause: Fettling knife dragged rather than pressed through the clay
rows.fix: Keep the knife vertical and cut with a rocking, downward pressure. Smooth rough edges with the needle tool at leather-hard.

rows.problem: PVA turns cloudy on the textured surface
rows.cause: Coat applied too thickly or pooled in deep texture recesses
rows.fix: Apply PVA with a brush, working it into the texture recesses and then brushing lightly back over to remove excess. Allow each coat to dry fully clear before adding the next.
